MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against standard dense embedding baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe) and a sememe-specific prior (MST), which is appropriate and relevant for its sememe-enhanced embeddings. However, comparisons appear limited to a single primary dataset (Chinese Word Analogy/word similarity tasks) and the summary does not report statistical significance testing or multiple random runs to ensure robustness, nor does it include more recent/contextual baselines that would strengthen claims of improvement. The paper does acknowledge limitations of its sememe modeling and discusses related work, but I deduct a point for the narrow empirical scope and lack of rigorous significance/ablation reporting in the comparisons. | B(4): The paper compares against standard and relevant baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe) and a cognate sememe-aware baseline (MST), uses the same dataset and metrics (Chinese analogy, Spearman/accuracy/mean rank), and reports hyperparameters—indicating largely fair experimental conditions. The authors analyze differences between their SSA/SAC/SAT variants and conventional skip-gram and include thoughtful discussion of limitations and related work. Minor omissions remain: no mention of code release for reproducibility and it’s unclear whether newer sense-aware or contextual embedding baselines were included, so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against standard dense embedding baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe) and a sememe-specific prior (MST), which is appropriate and relevant for its sememe-enhanced embeddings. However, comparisons appear limited to a single primary dataset (Chinese Word Analogy/word similarity tasks) and the summary does not report statistical significance testing or multiple random runs to ensure robustness, nor does it include more recent/contextual baselines that would strengthen claims of improvement. The paper does acknowledge limitations of its sememe modeling and discusses related work, but I deduct a point for the narrow empirical scope and lack of rigorous significance/ablation reporting in the comparisons.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against standard and relevant baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe) and a cognate sememe-aware baseline (MST), uses the same dataset and metrics (Chinese analogy, Spearman/accuracy/mean rank), and reports hyperparameters—indicating largely fair experimental conditions. The authors analyze differences between their SSA/SAC/SAT variants and conventional skip-gram and include thoughtful discussion of limitations and related work. Minor omissions remain: no mention of code release for reproducibility and it’s unclear whether newer sense-aware or contextual embedding baselines were included, so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is overall well organized and the three main model contributions (SSA, SAC, SAT) are stated early and traced clearly through method and experiments; writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is thorough. Notation and definitions appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables effectively support evaluations on word similarity and analogy. I deduct one point because, from the summary, some methodological simplifications (e.g., ignoring sememe internal structure) and absence of explicit code availability are not fully contextualized in presentation, leaving minor clarity gaps for reproducibility and practical use. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from motivation through three sememe-enhanced Skip-gram variants (SSA, SAC, SAT) to experiments, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and definitions appear consistent (sememe/sense/word embeddings, attention variants) and hyperparameters are reported, which aids reproducibility. Figures, tables and case studies are informative for comparing against strong baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST) on similarity and analogy benchmarks, and the related work coverage is thorough. A minor shortcoming is the lack of released code and a few places where the ignored internal sememe structure could have been more explicitly justified, preventing a perfect clarity score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is overall well organized and the three main model contributions (SSA, SAC, SAT) are stated early and traced clearly through method and experiments; writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is thorough. Notation and definitions appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables effectively support evaluations on word similarity and analogy. I deduct one point because, from the summary, some methodological simplifications (e.g., ignoring sememe internal structure) and absence of explicit code availability are not fully contextualized in presentation, leaving minor clarity gaps for reproducibility and practical use.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from motivation through three sememe-enhanced Skip-gram variants (SSA, SAC, SAT) to experiments, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and definitions appear consistent (sememe/sense/word embeddings, attention variants) and hyperparameters are reported, which aids reproducibility. Figures, tables and case studies are informative for comparing against strong baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST) on similarity and analogy benchmarks, and the related work coverage is thorough. A minor shortcoming is the lack of released code and a few places where the ignored internal sememe structure could have been more explicitly justified, preventing a perfect clarity score.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the work is a meaningful engineering extension that integrates HowNet sememes and attention into Skip-gram variants, producing a practical and interpretable improvement rather than a radical theoretical advance. Given the reliance on a single lexical resource, lack of deeper formal analysis, and the incremental nature relative to well-studied embedding methods, a moderate originality score of 3 appropriately reflects the paper’s non-trivial but not groundbreaking contribution.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers a moderately novel application by jointly learning sememe, sense and word embeddings and proposing three sememe-encoded Skip-gram variants (SSA, SAC, SAT) that use attention for sense/sememe selection—this is a non-trivial engineering extension. However, the core problem (word representation learning) and base technique (Skip-gram with sense/sememe supervision from HowNet) are well-studied, so the contribution is incremental rather than a fundamentally new perspective. From a theoretical purist view, the work lacks objective-level interpretation or formal analysis and heavily depends on a single lexical resource (HowNet), limiting perceived novelty and generalisability.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper brings a notable and practical novelty by integrating HowNet sememe annotations directly into joint sememe–sense–word embedding learning and proposing three sememe-encoded variants of Skip-gram (SSA, SAC, SAT) that use attention for sense/sememe selection. This is more than a straightforward application of prior embeddings because it operationalizes linguistic sememe information and attention into the learning objective, clearly differentiated from standard Skip-gram/CBOW/GloVe baselines and the MST baseline. While the overall problem (learning improved word representations) is well-studied, the use of sememe resources and the specific attention-based mechanisms constitute a non-trivial extension that would interest practitioners seeking interpretable, resource-informed embeddings.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a core representation-learning problem and is of clear relevance to the NLP/ML community, with sensible methods and empirical gains on standard intrinsic tasks. I rate it a strong but not perfect fit: while the approach and results make it appropriate for a top venue, concerns about reliance on a single resource (HowNet), limited evaluation breadth and lack of broader theoretical justification or reproducibility artifacts temper a maximal score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP/representation-learning topic (sememe-enhanced embeddings using HowNet and attention-augmented Skip-gram variants) that is within scope for a top NLP/ML venue and targets the right audience. However, from a principled-reviewer perspective its fit is borderline: the work leans heavily on a single lexical resource (HowNet) with no discussion of coverage or generalisability, evaluations are limited to one Chinese analogy dataset and case studies, and there is no sign of theoretical/objective-level justification or rigorous statistical testing across seeds—weakening its general appeal for a top conference without stronger theoretical motivation, broader evaluation, and reproducibility artifacts.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses core representation learning questions by integrating a linguistic resource (HowNet) to produce interpretable sememe/sense/word embeddings and demonstrates consistent empirical improvements on standard intrinsic tasks (word similarity and analogy) against strong baselines (skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST). The methods (three sememe-aware Skip-gram variants with attention) and evaluations (Spearman, accuracy, mean rank) match community expectations, and the work speaks directly to NLP researchers interested in semantically informed embeddings and sense modeling. The only minor concerns are the ignored internal sememe relations and lack of mentioned code release, but these do not detract from topical fit or relevance.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the proposed sememe-augmented Skip-gram variants are clearly presented and empirically improve embeddings against reasonable baselines, and the mathematical formulations are adequate for replication. However, limitations in evaluation scope (single dataset/language), lack of statistical significance testing, missing comprehensive ablations, and the simplifying assumption of ignoring internal sememe structure temper the strength of the claims. Overall the method is technically sound and promising but would benefit from broader and more rigorous evaluation, so a score of 4 is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and provides experimental evidence that sememe-enhanced Skip-gram variants (SSA, SAC, SAT) improve embedding quality, but the support is primarily empirical on a limited set of tasks/dataset and lacks statistical significance testing across seeds or datasets. There is no nontrivial mathematical derivation or theoretical justification for the attention/sense-selection mechanisms beyond architectural description, and the ignored internal sememe structure is a substantive unsupported modelling assumption. Methodologically the baselines are reasonable but the absence of comprehensive ablations isolating each component, reliance on a single lexical resource (HowNet) without discussion of coverage/bias, and missing significance analyses leave the conclusions only partially justified.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states and supports its main technical claims with a plausible joint sememe–sense–word learning framework and three concrete sememe-augmented Skip-gram variants, and reports consistent improvements on word similarity and analogy against reasonable baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST). Mathematical formulations appear standard and complete for Skip-gram extensions, and hyperparameters are reported, which aids reproducibility, but code is not provided. Minor concerns remain: evaluation seems focused on a single Chinese analogy dataset and no statistical significance testing is mentioned, and the simplifying assumption of ignoring sememe internal structure is an explicit limitation that could affect some claims. Overall the methods and reported results are technically sound though broader evaluation and released code would strengthen the evidence.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a useful empirical contribution by injecting sememe knowledge into embeddings and demonstrating consistent gains, but its broader impact is limited by reliance on a single resource/language (HowNet/Chinese), lack of formal analysis and ablation/statistical rigor, and limited evidence of generalisability or provided code. Given these strengths and clear practical promise but constrained scope and theoretical depth, a moderate impact score of 3 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a meaningful problem—integrating sememe annotations into word/sense embeddings—and demonstrates empirical gains on Chinese word similarity and analogy tasks using three sememe-aware Skip-gram variants (SSA, SAC, SAT). However, from a theoretical-purist perspective its impact is limited by the absence of objective-level interpretation or formal analysis of the proposed models, reliance on a single lexical resource (HowNet) and a single-language dataset, and no indication of rigorous statistical testing or broad ablations isolating each component. These factors make the work a useful empirical contribution that may spur follow-up studies, but unlikely to fundamentally change how researchers think about representation learning without stronger theoretical justification and evidence of generalisability.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—injecting explicit lexical-semantic knowledge (HowNet sememes) into embeddings—and produces interpretable sememe/sense components and attention-based methods (SAC/SAT) that yield consistent gains on similarity and analogy tasks. From my perspective as an applied semantics reviewer, the work credibly demonstrates that sememe supervision can improve representations and aid sense disambiguation, which should influence researchers who build linguistically-informed embeddings. The reliance on HowNet (Chinese) and ignoring sememe internal structure limit immediate generality, and lack of mentioned code may slow adoption, but the approach opens clear directions for sememe-aware models and interpretable embedding design.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a meaningful empirical contribution showing sememe-aware models improve similarity and analogy performance and that related work and limitations are well-discussed. However, they also concur that evaluation is narrow (mostly Chinese tasks, single lexical resource), lacks thorough ablations, significance testing, and code release, which undermines reproducibility and the strength of empirical claims; these shortcomings justify a modest score of 3.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are adequate in showing improvements on word similarity and the Chinese word analogy benchmark and compare to sensible baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST), but evaluation is limited to a small set of tasks/datasets and there is no evidence of statistical significance testing across seeds. Ablations are weak: although three variants (SSA, SAC, SAT) are introduced, the paper lacks systematic component-level ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating attention, sememe aggregation, and other architectural choices. Related work is reported as thorough, and the authors do acknowledge the limitation of ignoring sememe relational structure, but reliance on a single lexical resource (HowNet) and no code release or multi-dataset validation harm reproducibility and generalisability.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid empirical evidence that sememe-aware models (SSA, SAC, SAT) improve word similarity and analogy performance versus reasonable baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST) and includes qualitative case studies for disambiguation, which aligns with my applied-semantics priorities. It reports hyperparameters and discusses a clear limitation (ignoring internal sememe relations), and related work coverage is thorough. However, experiments appear focused mainly on Chinese analogy/similarity and do not present thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of sememe attention vs. simple aggregation across settings) nor is code release mentioned, leaving some reproducibility/adoption gaps. Overall the evaluation is strong but has minor gaps that prevent it from being fully comprehensive.

main_claims: Jointly learns representations of sememes, senses and words using HowNet annotations.
Introduces three sememe-encoded extensions of Skip-gram (SSA, SAC, SAT) that use sememe information and attention for sense selection.
Sememe-enhanced models significantly outperform conventional baselines on word similarity and word analogy tasks and can aid word sense disambiguation (case studies).
_summary.methodology: Extends the Skip-gram framework by incorporating sememe annotations from HowNet to learn sememe, sense and word embeddings via three models—Simple Sememe Aggregation (SSA), Sememe Attention over Context (SAC) and Sememe Attention over Target (SAT)—using attention mechanisms to select senses/sememes in context.
metrics: Cosine similarity (used to rank word pairs)
limitations: For simplicity the internal structure and relations among sememes (hierarchical/relational annotations) are ignored — each sense is represented as an unstructured set of sememes.

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in representation learning with a strong focus on formal guarantees and principled model design; many years of experience publishing theoretical analyses of embedding methods.
traits: Penalises papers that present primarily empirical improvements without any theoretical justification or formal analysis of the model (e.g., no objective-level interpretation, convergence/expressivity statements).

Requires and deducts points if key empirical claims lack rigorous statistical significance testing across multiple random seeds/datasets (e.g., no confidence intervals or p-values for reported gains).
Penalises papers that do not include comprehensive ablation studies isolating each architectural/component contribution (e.g., attention vs. sememe usage vs. aggregation).
Downscores work that relies heavily on a single lexical resource (HowNet) without discussing coverage, biases, or generalisability to other languages/resources.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritises principled understanding and reproducibility; will accept empirical work only if accompanied by strong theoretical motivation, rigorous significance testing and thorough ablations.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Semantics Advocate
reviewer_b.background: Experienced NLP practitioner and reviewer focused on lexical semantics and practical embedding methods; has applied semantically-informed representations in multiple downstream systems.
traits: Rewards work that leverages linguistic resources (e.g., HowNet) to produce more interpretable and semantically-rich embeddings and that demonstrates concrete empirical gains on standard benchmarks.
Gives credit for models that produce interpretable components (sememe/sense vectors) and useful qualitative case studies showing improved disambiguation or semantic behavior.
Favourably scores papers that show consistent improvements across multiple intrinsic tasks (word similarity, analogy) and that include reasonable baselines (skip-gram, sense/embedding baselines).
Awards extra points if the authors release code or clear implementation details that enable adoption and reproducibility in practice.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and adoption-oriented: values clear empirical improvements, interpretability, and resourcefulness in leveraging linguistic knowledge to advance usable representations.
